title: Chloe Fineman Says ‘SNL’ Boss Lorne Michaels Was at World Cup Match When She Called to Say She Was Leaving the Show
description:
“Saturday Night Live” boss Lorne Michaels was a bit tied up when Chloe Fineman called to say she was leaving the show. 

	“In classic form, he was w
body:
“Saturday Night Live” boss Lorne Michaels was a bit tied up when Chloe Fineman called to say she was leaving the show. 

	“In classic form, he was watching England vs. Argentina,” Fineman told Variety at Monday night’s premiere of Apple TV’s “The Dink” in Los Angeles. “He was like, ‘I’ll call you back. I’m a the game.’ And I was like, ‘The soccer game?’ Then I made a joke, I was like, ‘I assume you’re rooting for England because you’re energetically very British.’ Then he called me back, and we had a really lovely chat.” 
	

Chloe Fineman says Lorne Michaels was at the World Cup when she called to tell him she was leaving #SNL:“He was like, ‘I’ll call you back. I’m at the soccer game.’ And I was like, ‘The soccer game? I assume you’re rooting for England because you’re energetically very British.’” pic.twitter.com/yXMV0zUP7r— Variety (@Variety) July 21, 2026


	Fineman also shared that when the news of her “SNL” exit went live, she was very emotional.


	“I cried when it was announced,” she said. “You really become a family [with the cast]… I wept when Colin Jost called me today, like, they really are my family. I’m happy that after seven years, I leave on a high note.” 

	Fineman announced on July 16 that she was exiting the show. “After 7 wonderful seasons at ‘SNL’ I have decided it’s time for my next chapter,” Fineman wrote in an Instagram post. She concluded, “It’s really hard to leave ‘SNL’ but it does feel like the right time. I’m going to miss it a lot. But the people who work there are my family and that place is my home, and I know I’ll never be too far away.”

Chloe Fineman says she cried after announcing she was leaving #SNL:“They really are like my best friends. So many people there. But it’s good to leave on a positive. You don’t want to stay past your expiration date.” pic.twitter.com/0W7uAJHVLW— Variety (@Variety) July 21, 2026


	The actor-comedian joined “SNL” in 2019. She quickly became known for her range of impressions, which included Jennifer Coolidge, Timothée Chalamet, Drew Barrymore, Britney Spears and JoJo Siwa.
	

Chloe Fineman says “we just scratched the surface” of her Melania Trump impression on #SNL.After announcing this week that she's leaving the sketch comedy series following seven seasons, Fineman says “you never know” when asked if she could return to play the First Lady during… pic.twitter.com/hniYeeG109— Variety (@Variety) July 21, 2026


	Fineman’s first post-“SNL” gig will be as a series regular role in the “Myron Bolitar” series currently in the works at Netflix. Series regulars will also include Jabari Banks (“Bel Air”) and Jamie McShane (“Task,” “Wednesday”). Ben McKenzie (“Everyone is Lying to You for Money,” “Gotham”) will appear in a recurring guest star role.

	Series leads include Colin Woodell, KJ Apa, and Diane Guerrero. Netflix originally announced the series pickup in May as part of their upfront presentation to advertisers. The show is inspired by Harlan Coben’s popular Myron Bolitar books, of which 12 have been published. The logline for the show states, “After an injury ends his NBA dreams, Myron Bolitar (Woodell) reinvents himself as a sports agent — using charm, smarts, and a ruthless partner to navigate the high-stakes and dirty world of sports, where saving his clients often means risking himself.”

	In addition to Fineman, the cast of “The Dink,” a sports comedy about pickleball, includes Jake Johnson, Ben Stiller, Christine Taylor, Patton Oswalt, Martin Kove, Mary Steenburgen and Ed Harris, as well as tennis legends Andy Roddick and John McEnroe.

	“The Dink” premieres on Apple TV on July 24.

schema.headline: Chloe Fineman Says ‘SNL’ Boss Lorne Michaels Was at World Cup Match When She Called to Say She Was Leaving the Show

title: Jon Stewart Says the World Cup Final Was the ‘Reverse Super Bowl’: ‘The Players Spoke Spanish and the Halftime Show Was in English’
description: On Monday night’s episode of “The Daily Show,” Jon Stewart gave his post-game thoughts about Sunday’s World Cup final match, which saw Spain win out
body:
On Monday night’s episode of “The Daily Show,” Jon Stewart gave his post-game thoughts about Sunday’s World Cup final match, which saw Spain win out over Argentina 1-0.

	“Of course, the World Cup ended yesterday, finito, with Spain defeating Argentina 1-0 on the pitch, in the footy,” Stewart said at the top of the show. “The Spain-Argentina final was like a reverse Super Bowl, where the players spoke Spanish, and the halftime show was in English.”
	

	The World Cup final halftime show, the first in the tournament’s history, featured Madonna, Justin Bieber and the Muppets. Stewart took note of “Sesame Street’s” The Count, who was there to celebrate the match along with Kermit and Miss Piggy.


	“You got all the Muppets to choose from, why would in God’s name make the vampire work during the day?” Stewart joked. “Not only is it in the bright glare of the afternoon sun, but for most of the game, the score was 0-0! You’re taunting this poor bastard! ‘I sat in traffic for one, two, three hours for this.’”

	Stewart then, of course, shifted his attention to President Donald Trump’s presence at the game. He honed in on an awkward moment where Trump lingered on stage to be part of the trophy hoist, a moment usually reserved for “just the players.”

	“What the fuck are you doing on the stage?” Stewart exclaimed. “It’s for the players! Goddamn! Why must everything be tainted by the overinflated ego of Cankles Muldoon? Why?”

	Check out the entire monologue below.

schema.headline: Jon Stewart Says the World Cup Final Was the ‘Reverse Super Bowl’: ‘The Players Spoke Spanish and the Halftime Show Was in English’

title: Peter Lassally, Legendary Late Night Producer for Johnny Carson, David Letterman and Craig Ferguson, Dies at 93
description: Peter Lassally, the dean of late night producers who worked with Johnny Carson on the “Tonight Show,” David Letterman on “Late Show” and Craig Fergu
body:
Peter Lassally, the dean of late night producers who worked with Johnny Carson on the “Tonight Show,” David Letterman on “Late Show” and Craig Ferguson on “The Late Late Show,” has died. He was 93.

	Lassally was long dubbed the “host whisperer” for helping steer some of the biggest franchises of all time in late night. He worked with Carson until that host retired in 1992; after that, he joined forces with Letterman, who had hoped to take the “Tonight” mantle but was famously passed over for the gig. That’s when Lassally, joining Letterman’s Worldwide Pants shingle, was instrumental in helping Letterman decide to leave NBC and create an entirely new “Late Show” franchise at CBS. 
	

	Under Letterman’s deal with CBS, Worldwide Pants also programmed the 12:37 a.m. slot with “The Late Late Show” — which Lassally executive produced in its first incarnation with Tom Snyder. When it later went to Craig Kilborn, Lassally semi-retired, but when Craig Ferguson took over in 2005, Lassally served as the show’s executive producer.


	“Peter had two loves in his life that lasted more than a half-century, the first with his lovely wife, Alice, and the second with the world of talk television,” said Michael Naidus, who worked with Lassally on Ferguson’s “Late Late Show” as showrunner. “Each of those love affairs was a two-way street.  Quite literally, hundreds of millions of people owe their late-night smiles and laughs to this beautiful soul, whose gift for finding extraordinary talent and honing it is unmatched and unrivaled.  

	“And all the while, Peter took such joy from the moments he helped create, that his grin was as ubiquitous as his daily sliced apple,” he added. “Whatever little I gave to the format is because he was kind and patient enough to teach it to me. It’s traditional to say ‘he will be missed’ in these circumstances, but the truth is Peter was and is irreplaceable.”
	

	Lassally retired in 2015, as Ferguson departed “The Late Late Show,” overseeing guest hosts before James Corden took over.

	In a 2015 Variety profile, then-CBS Entertainment chairman Nina Tassler referred to Lassally as “the strong, silent type,” with an unerring ability to “diagnose problems with surgical precision” and a nurturing attitude that elicited the best from the big personalities with whom he’s dealt, as well as with the show’s staffs. “He’s kind of ego-less,” Tassler noted. “There’s a comfort he derives from not wanting or needing to be in the spotlight.”

	An NBC spokesperson shared, “When Johnny Carson and David Letterman needed counsel and an honest opinion, they immediately went to Peter Lassally, the professor of late night. His relationship to each of these giants was immeasurable and both of them would undoubtedly say his contribution to their success was so impactful that it would be difficult to put in words. A quiet man whose influence and stature spoke volumes, Peter’s brilliance was in shaping what we watch, how we watch and why late night was, and remains, vitally significant.”

	Lassally was born in Hamburg, Germany, in 1932 to a Jewish family that later had to flee to the Netherlands with the rise of Nazi Germany. While there, Lassally’s sister went to school with Anne Frank, and he, his mother and sister were imprisoned in the Westerbork transit camp and later in the Theresienstadt concentration camp. After surviving the Holocaust, Lassally and his family moved to New York in 1947.

	Lassally was a part of TV from the very beginning, joining the NBC page program at age 19 and serving as a Radio City Music Hall usher alongside his pal Regis Philbin. He worked in radio, then moved to Arthur Godfrey’s popular program. 

	He joined “The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson” in the 1970s, rising to executive producer until Carson’s retirement. From there, he moved to “Late Night with David Letterman,” and then helped establish “Late Show” with Letterman. (That franchise, which later continued under Stephen Colbert, recently wrapped following CBS’ surprise cancellation.)

	Lassally’s final act in late night was identifying comedian Craig Ferguson (then known mostly for his role on “Drew Carey”) and transforming him into a talk show host — which ultimately, as Ferguson grew into the role, became a critically acclaimed, unique late night outpost.
	

	Wrote Ferguson on Instagram: “Peter Lassally was the best boss I ever had. His kindness generosity and skill were incredible. He taught me how to do a job I was deeply unqualified for and he changed my life beyond measure. I will always be grateful for what he did for me and my family. Thank you Peter.”

	Lassally won two Emmy awards over the years, while discovering and nurturing multiple generations of talent.

	He was preceded in death by his wife, Alice Lassally, and is survived by his daughter, Andrea Gibbin (Chris Gibbin); his son, 3 Arts’ Tom Lassally (Romi Lassally); and his four grandchildren, Walker Gibbin, Eloise Gibbin, Annabel Lassally, and Owen Lassally.

schema.headline: Peter Lassally, Legendary Late Night Producer for Johnny Carson, David Letterman and Craig Ferguson, Dies at 93

title: ‘Sherlock’ Creator Steven Moffat Set as International Guest of Serielizados – Barcelona Series Festival (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Currently priming “Number 10,” one of the most awaited of British TV series, “Sherlock” creator and “Doctor Who” showrunner Steven Moffat will atten
body:
Currently priming “Number 10,” one of the most awaited of British TV series, “Sherlock” creator and “Doctor Who” showrunner Steven Moffat will attend Serielizados-Barcelona Series Festival as its headline international guest. He will be joined producer Sue Vertue, producer of “Mr. Bean,” “Sherlock” and “Number 10” and CEO of Hartswood Films, the U.K.’s longest-established scripted producer, also behind ITVX and ITV1 hit “Douglas Is Cancelled.” Hartswood Films was majority acquired by ITV Studios in 2024. 
	

	Serielizados has yet to reveal what Moffat and Vertue will talk about. If a Series Mania masterclass is anything to go on, their session in Barcelona will most certainly be funny. 


	“Real life is funny – drama is a lie,” Moffat said at Serielizados in Lille. “You can’t be too funny, too sexy or too pretty. Always go for funny. Humor is truth on speed, that’s what it is. It’s insight with velocity.”

	British humor is one of the things the British can be very good at, and one of its television industry’s main export values.

	Serielizados is ever more aligned with the Catalan TV industry while still attracting the Spanish premieres of some of the most talked-about international series of the year – 2025’s festival was won, for example, by Norway’s “A Better Man” and Canada’s “Empathy” – the conference strand at Serielizados industry arm, Serielizados Pro, generated a high-quality debate energized top executives from Nordic broadcasters SVT, DR and YLE, plus also reps from BBC Studios, Arte France, Germany’s Beta Film and Paris-based Slot Machine. 
	

	In-person attendance soared last year 80% to 16,000, while Serielizados’ online version, hosted by upscale SVOD service Filmin, hit 194,000 viewers.

	‘In Vitro,’ ‘Episòdic’

	In 2026, running Oct. 19-28, Serielizados will open with “In Vitro,” backed by Catalan public broadcaster 3Cat and HBO Max and set to debut this fall. 



	

	



	Created by Marc Crehuet and Alejandra Guimerà, and directed by Creheut (“Pop Ràpid,” “The One-Eyed King,” !Mirror, Mirror”), it turns on Blanca, 37, a biologist, new to a fertility clinic where she confronts the needs and risks of her clients and her own as well. Beta Film picked up international distribution rights for“In Vitro” ahead of Series Mania.  

	Serielizados closes with an experiement, the first season of “Episòdic: El principi d’Arquimedes,” a six-part anthology series, each episode lasting 15 minutes, made by 38 audiovisual students from universities, film schools and educational institutions. 

	The series is directed by Ainhoa Lastra (“La nit del foc,” at the Universitat Pompeu Fabra,  and Andrea Morales (Paraules d’amor) and Carlos R. Haro (“Cinc minuts més, si us plau”). The series is written by Daniel Durán Pous – a producer on “Unicorni” with two shorts to his credit, “Hipolit” and “L’ultim tren” – adapting the “The Arquimedes Principle,” from playwright Josep María Miró. An alleged child-abuse drama which cleverly plays with audiences own sense of prejudice, the work is hailed in its native Catalonia as a modern Catalan classic.



	

					Shooting ‘Episòdic’
	



	‘Segunda Temporada,’ ‘Nissaga de poder,’ ‘Literaturium de séries‘

	Serielizados will also showcase “Segunda Temporada,” the latest Filmin Original from Spain’s upscale SVOD streaming service, turning on Paula, a once bestselling generational voice who, after writers’ block, is reduced to ghosting the bio of a washed up trap music legend, just out of prison, and maybe or maybe not feeling redemption. Created by Miguel Angel Blanca, behind breakout Filmin hit “Autodefensa,” “Segunda Temporada” will contend in Serielizados national competition.



	

					‘Segunda Temporada’
	



	The TV festival will also showcase the 30th anniversary of 3Cat daily soap “Nissaga de poder,” a milestone in its drive for ambitious originals, and “Literaturium de séries,” an exclusive live edition of the comedy podcast reviewing the supposed but fictional best TV series of the year.
	

	Mind The Gap 

	Serielizados also announced Tuesday the second edition of Mind the Gap, its project pitching showcase which will take place Oct. 21, forming part of Serielizados PRO. Expanded to 11 selected titles, with its call for submissions now open and extended through Aug. 17, eight will be chosen from an open call and three tapped from winners at Germany’s Seriencamp Co-Production Pitch and Spain’s ECAM Forum and Iberseries & Platino Industria – an indication of how European events are working together to get projects in front of its top commissioners, production executives and sales agents who make up Mind the Gap’s jury. 

	Participants can also expect press coverage. Winners at Mind the Gap’s inaugural edition last year were led by best series laureate “Fortune Cookie,” a low-fi sci-fi drama thriller set in Madrid’s Usera Chinatown, created by Adrián Saba (“The Erection of Toribio Bardelli”) and produced by Kubik Films, Newen Studios Spanish banner headed by brothers Fernando, Jorge and Alberto Sánchez-Cabezudo.   

	“A comedy about two Gen Z misfits dodging eviction, chasing dreams, flipping the finger to adulthood and to the glittering city [Barcelona] that’s leaving them behind,” said producer Anna M. Bofarull, “Precarity Fair,” created by Eli Cruz and Mika Cilindro and produced by KaBoGa, won Mind the Gap’s Konga Music Award.

	In a further move, Serielizados has released its poster with a slogan, “An Eye for It,” which, it says, “emphasizes the role of its programming team in filtering and curating the ever-growing number of television series, helping audiences discover the most exciting and distinctive titles in an increasingly crowded landscape.”

	Serielizados has indeed often shown impecable taste in its lineup selection and top prizes, a jury recognising Sweden’s “Pressure Point” in 2024, for instance, as best international series. Plaudits in 2025 in the national competition went to “The Anatomy of a Moment” and “Jakarta.”

schema.headline: ‘Sherlock’ Creator Steven Moffat Set as International Guest of Serielizados – Barcelona Series Festival (EXCLUSIVE)

title: Ed Harris Felt ‘Misled’ by ‘Dutton Ranch’ About ‘Inconsequential’ Role: ‘Get Me the F— Out of Here’ (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Ed Harris does not sound satisfied with his role on “Dutton Ranch.” At the Monday night premiere of Apple TV’s “The Dink,” Harris told Variety‘s Mar
body:
Ed Harris does not sound satisfied with his role on “Dutton Ranch.” At the Monday night premiere of Apple TV’s “The Dink,” Harris told Variety‘s Marc Malkin that he felt “misled” about the importance of his role in the “Yellowstone” spinoff, and that halfway through filming Season 1, he was telling his team to “get me the fuck out of here.”

	“Prior to signing on for it, I was talked to quite a bit about what the season was, and what my character was going to do — and that I was one of the four main characters,” Harris said. “And that wasn’t really the case.”
	

	Spokespeople for Paramount, series creator Taylor Sheridan and 101 Studios did not immediately respond to Variety‘s request for comment.


	Harris said he expressed his discontent, but did not name Sheridan. “I said, ‘I’m feeling underused and inconsequential,’ and they kind of went, ‘Oh,'” Harris added. “I’m being told my character will have a little bit more to do [in Season 2]. I was just a little frustrated, to tell you the truth.” 

Ed Harris says he felt “misled” about his “inconsequential” role on “Dutton Ranch,” and that halfway through filming Season 1, he was telling his team to “get me the fuck out of here.”“Prior to signing on for it, I was talked to quite a bit about what the season was, and what… pic.twitter.com/jwafO8ZUih— Variety (@Variety) July 21, 2026


	Harris explained that when he actually watched “Dutton Ranch,” he thought it was “pretty good,” and he felt his character was “relatively important to the story.” However, during filming, “it didn’t feel that way.” 

	On “Dutton Ranch,” Harris plays Everett McKinney, a good-humored veterinarian. Harris is currently signed on for two seasons. While Harris suggested he was ready to walk away from “Dutton Ranch,” it is unclear if he is actually looking to exit the show.

	The “Apollo 13” star then recalled filming a scene where his character sings in a bar, only to have the moment cut from the show. Harris said that “really pissed him off.” 
	

	“So there’s this scene in there … I’m talking to [Beth Dutton] at the bar, and [Rip Wheeler] comes in and says, ‘OK, Everett, come on, it’s your turn,” Harris said. “I walk up to the mic, and they cut it. They cut the song out. I’m going, ‘Fuck you, people.'”

	Harris remembered the production telling him they cut the moment because it was “too ‘up’ for the dark ending of that episode.” But Harris said, at the time, he felt like that was “enough reason to say I’m voiding my contract.”

	“Dutton Ranch” launched its series premiere on Paramount+ on May 15. The show follows Beth Dutton (Kelly Reilly) and Rip Wheeler (Cole Hauser) as they leave Montana to build their own ranch in South Texas. Other cast members include Finn Little, Juan Pablo Raba, Jai Courtney, J.R. Villarreal, Marc Menchaca, Natalie Alyn Lind and Annette Bening.

schema.headline: Ed Harris Felt ‘Misled’ by ‘Dutton Ranch’ About ‘Inconsequential’ Role: ‘Get Me the F— Out of Here’ (EXCLUSIVE)

title: LISTEN: Christopher Nolan Gets Moviegoers Out in Force for the Trojan Horse
description: On today’s episode of “Daily Variety” podcast, in our Box Office segment, Variety’s Rebecca Rubin breaks down the spectacular $264 million worldwide
body:
On today’s episode of “Daily Variety” podcast, in our Box Office segment, Variety’s Rebecca Rubin breaks down the spectacular $264 million worldwide opening for Universal Pictures’ “The Odyssey.” It couldn’t have come at a better time for Imax, the large-format exhibitor that delivered more than 50% of the opening weekend receipts.

	Anticipation for Nolan’s follow-up to 2023’s “Oppenheimer” was so strong that there was little doubt that the $250 million production would open big. But the final figures demonstrates just how solidly “The Odyssey” was able to mobilize audiences across the world.
	

	“What’s most impressive is the fact that this movie had such a big opening weekend for an R-rated sword and sandal epic. Christopher Nolan is really the only director who could pull something like this off at the budget that he did and justify the spend from the studio,” Rubin says. “He has really solidified himself as a brand at the box office, and he’s one of the very, very rare directors today who can get people to go out to the movies just because he is putting out a new film. It doesn’t even matter what the genre is, what type of film it is. People just want to see whatever he is making next.”

	Rubin notes that the opening weekend audience was about 55% men, and a big chunk of the crowd was on the younger side for this genre of film.

	“People have been joking that it’s the quintessential dad movie,” Rubin says. “But what was pretty significant to me is that 50% of audiences were between the ages of 18 and 34, which means he really got the younger crowds in there. If this was in the hands of a different director, I think it could be a movie that skewed much older. Love Matt Damon — he is more of a Gen X star. A lot of the cast is millennials, and so they might not have gotten that younger crowd. But [Nolan] really did incentivize all audiences to go. That is the difference between having a decent opening weekend and a phenomenal opening weekend — he was able to get all ages. I think is going to really help the film in the coming weeks.”
	

	Listen to Daily Variety on iHeartPodcasts, Apple Podcasts, Variety’s YouTube Podcast channel, Amazon Music, Spotify and other podcast platforms.

schema.headline: LISTEN: Christopher Nolan Gets Moviegoers Out in Force for the Trojan Horse

title: Sharon Stone on Quitting Weed and Stopping a Stroke Medication Despite Doctors’ Orders: ‘It Was Like Going Off of F—ing Heroin’
description:
Sharon Stone is done smoking weed.

	Her journey to cut ties with cannabis began when she was put on several medications after she suffered a near-f
body:
Sharon Stone is done smoking weed.

	Her journey to cut ties with cannabis began when she was put on several medications after she suffered a near-fatal stroke in 2001.

	“I got off one of my medications that I was on for my stroke last September, and they said I couldn’t go off of it,” Stone tells me. “They said, ‘You can’t go off of it. You can never go off of it.’ I’m like, ‘I’m going off it,’ and it was like going off of fucking heroin. I was sick as a frigging dog for four and a half months. I just thought, ‘I want my life back.’ I had gotten off the majority of the drugs over the years that they had put me on. But I was like, ‘I want a drug-free life.’”
	

	She continues, “Once I got off that, I decided I’m going to quit smoking pot. And because I think they put so much stuff in it now — they really do — when I stopped smoking pot, I also got sick. It’s not like you’re pulling it out of the ground anymore. It’s not like when we were kids. I got pretty sick for a month. I went to my neurologist, and he said, ‘Sharon, I’ve had people dying in the emergency room from marijuana.’”


	Stone is sitting poolside at her Beverly Hills estate on a Monday afternoon. Just minutes before, I watched her paint on a large canvas in a guest house she’s turned into an art studio. At the same time, photographer Tyler Chick is shooting her for digital magazine Nineteen92. The day is also being chronicled by a group of young people from Social Network Studios, a social media management and branding company founded by her eldest son, Roan, that runs Stone’s social media.
	

	“I had four or five million followers [on Instagram], but not that much engagement,” Stone says. Roan overhauled her feed. “Now, I have millions in engagement.”

	But that also meant Roan had to modify who she was following. “People wrote me nasty letters. Why am I not following them anymore?” Stone says. “People got outraged that they were not being followed anymore, as if like this was a real relationship. As if I had a personal relationship with someone I was following on Instagram, someone I had never met.”

	While Stone’s painting career has taken off over the last few years, the art form isn’t new to her. The Pennsylvania native studied fine arts in college before she dropped out to pursue a modeling career. “I went to New York with 50 bucks and a suitcase,” she says.

	The creative spark was reignited during the COVID lockdown. What began as a few canvases and some paints in her bedroom turned into a sometimes around-the-clock obsession. Before she knew it, Stone transformed her bedroom into a makeshift art studio. “I moved all the furniture out of my bedroom. Before very long, I gave away my bedroom furniture and gotten a small bed,” Stone recalls. “I was living in like this French artist loft with this bed in front of the fireplace. I had this beautiful bedroom, but I put nails in the wall like I wasn’t living in this gorgeous house. I just put nails in this beautiful wall. Started buying bigger canvases. Brought a big table up there. Threw sheets on the floor. Months went by, months and months.”



	

					Stone paints in her studio.
									Alexander Moura
	



	Her sons – Roan and his brothers, Laird and Quinn – became worried and intervened. “They come in and they say to me, ‘Mom, we just think that you should wear a bra.'” Stone says. “I’m like, ‘This is the same conversation my dad had with me in the 70s.’ Didn’t work then. Not going to work now. Thanks for your concern. Have a lovely day.”

	They were able to persuade Stone to move her painting to the guest house. “I’m selling my work in galleries all around the world now,” she says.



	

					Stone poses for photographer Tyler Chick and creative director Andrew Matarazzo.
									Alexander Moura
	



	She also returned to modeling in June by walking a runway for the first time in 33 years as the closing model of the Vetements menswear show in Paris. “I really liked meeting the designer [Guram Gvasalia] because he’s very talented, and I think very clever, and he’s doing something which is to me quite punk,” Stone says. “And you know, I was actually around for punk, so I like punk a lot. I truly identify with punk.”
	

	She also couldn’t help but think of her early days modeling in Europe. “When I was 19, I had a 38-year-old boyfriend who was a designer, a silk designer, and I knew all of the designers because of him,” Stone recalls. “We traveled all over the world together. We dated on and off for 30 years. We’re still best friends.”

	Was marriage ever considered? “He would have married me, but I did not want to live in Europe,” Stone explains. “I knew I was going to be an actress. I knew what my destiny was. I really knew when I was little that this is what was going to happen to me. It wasn’t just all about the acting. I knew that I was going to be super famous, and that I had many things I was meant to do. I just knew it.”

	In the last year or so, Stone’s time in front of the camera includes appearances in “Euphoria,” the action comedy “Nobody 2” and the indie dramedy “In Memoriam.”

	Stone hopes a Broadway debut is in her future. She tells me she tried to secure the rights for a stage production of “All About Eve.” “I could be a good Margo Channing,” she says of the role of the aging Broadway star originated by Bette Davis in the 1950 film. “I mean, in my sleep. Let’s go. Come on. When she goes, ‘I hate men.’”



	

					An outtake from Stone’s Nineteen92 shoot by Alexander Moura.
	



	Stone says she’s been offered Broadway roles, but nothing ever felt right. She also revealed to me that she recently pitched an idea to her agent. “I was like, ‘Can we get ‘Mame’ and reset it in the punk era?” she says. “I could work at a department store in New York and get a biracial kid and be Mame in the punk era. The kid could have a mohawk. I would have bleached blonde Blondie hair. I could get Debbie Harry to do the music and Billy Idol.”

	Another idea brewing is a gender swap adaptation of “La Cage Aux Folles,” the Tony-winning musical about a gay couple operating a drag club who try to go back into the closet for just one night when the son of one of the men brings his conservative future in-laws to dinner. “’La Cage’ with two lesbians,” Stone says. “I play the lipstick lesbian and you find a big butch lesbian who has to fem it up.”

	But then she adds laughing, “I tell people these ideas and they just look at me and are like, ‘What the fuck are you taking?’”
	

	But as we now know, Stone is hardly taking anything these days. In fact, she says, “This has been a period for me in this last year of really reclaiming my life.”

schema.headline: Sharon Stone on Quitting Weed and Stopping a Stroke Medication Despite Doctors’ Orders: ‘It Was Like Going Off of F—ing Heroin’

title: ‘I’ve Been Scammed Left and Right’: Demand for ‘Odyssey’ Tickets Gives Rise to Sketchy Scalpers
description:
Elijahturner011 had a simple question: “Can someone release a ticket to me, please?”

	“I’ve been trying to get a ticket forever. I’ve been scammed
body:
Elijahturner011 had a simple question: “Can someone release a ticket to me, please?”

	“I’ve been trying to get a ticket forever. I’ve been scammed left and right,” they wrote on July 20 in the Reddit post that followed. “I just want one ticket to 70mm … it’s hurting my heart that I can’t just get one ticket for one day, and I’m hearing some people didn’t even show up to the movie. So please if you’re not sure or planning to not go, drop your ticket to me, please. I’m calling on Zeus himself.”
	

	This is just one of the desperate posts from dozens of moviegoers that have popped up on the subreddit r/NYCmovies, a place dedicated to discussing “moviegoing in New York City — AMC, Regal, Film Forum, IFC Center, Film at Lincoln Center, N.Y. Film Festival and more!” And even though they don’t all name the movie, it’s clear what the treasure they crave is: A ticket to see “The Odyssey” in Imax 70mm.


	While some New York City moviegoers are lucky enough to be able to experience Nolan’s film in his preferred format, screenings are sold out to an unprecedented degree. Wheelchair spots are all that remain for the showtimes at AMC Lincoln Square 13, one of just 41 Imax 70mm screens worldwide, and the only one in New York City.

	The sold-out Lincoln Square Imax 70mm screenings include all the 2 a.m. showings, which continue through Aug. 2, and all other listed showtimes, which run through August 12. A spokesperson for AMC declined to comment when asked if more showtimes were going to be added soon.

	On the front page of r/NYCmovies, there’s a clearly listed rule: No Scalping of Tickets. And yet scores of interlopers have dropped by the page in order to try and score a miracle — or even have more nefarious plans of ripping off cinephiles.
	

	Tessa, one of the mods of the page who has been working to protect the readers, tells Variety that the community has also been working to keep scalpers and scammers at bay. But it’s tricky, given the anonymity Reddit can afford.

	“People are very quick to report if someone is trying to sell them tickets above face value (scalping),” she says. “Anytime we get a report of someone doing that, we ban their account. However, often these scalpers, once they are discovered, will delete their own accounts and will make new ones. So it can be hard to successfully ban them completely. There are also users actively offering to pay above face value (which other users will also report). I’m sure there are some users quietly and willingly paying above face value. The worst thing are users pretending to have tickets they don’t have at all. Again, once reported, we ban, but they often just make new accounts. We are warning people to be careful and check post history, get proof of tickets, if possible do an in-person exchange (if you print the ticket on a kiosk you know you have a legit ticket that can no longer be double sold or refunded), and if you do an online transaction, turn on PayPal’s Goods & Services protection feature. Most legit users with extra tickets just refund them and will notify the sub when they are dropping the extra tickets.”

	Although the Reddit community is looking after its own, there are plenty of other places scalpers can strike. The $33 tickets have bids starting at $69 for Lincoln Square screenings on eBay, all the way up to $1,200 for a pair of seats on July 22.

	Perhaps a better idea for New Yorkers? Head upstate to Cinemark Tinseltown Rochester, a six-hour drive from midtown that has tickets available for showtimes this week in Imax 70mm. After all, it can’t take as long to get there as it took Odysseus to get back to Ithaca.

	Additional reporting by Matt Minton.

schema.headline: ‘I’ve Been Scammed Left and Right’: Demand for ‘Odyssey’ Tickets Gives Rise to Sketchy Scalpers

title: The Emmys ‘F—ed This Up’: Streamers and Networks Are Outraged as Five Categories Get Cut From the Telecast
description:
Bad move, Emmys. Just about everyone hates it.

	On Friday, Variety exclusively revealed the Television Academy’s plan to thin out the categories ha
body:
Bad move, Emmys. Just about everyone hates it.

	On Friday, Variety exclusively revealed the Television Academy’s plan to thin out the categories handed out during its primetime telecast on Sept. 14, after the Board of Governors voted to shift a hodgepodge of five races to the first weekend of ceremonies, once known as the Creative Arts Emmys. Now it’s all one big Emmys.

	The five categories: writing for a variety series; supporting actor and supporting actress in a limited or anthology series or movie; directing for a limited or anthology series or movie; and writing for a limited or anthology series or movie. All are scheduled to be handed out on the second night, Sunday, Sept. 6.
	

	There are plenty of reasons this is a terrible idea. Let’s start with the logistics.

	By our count, about nine additional nominees (along with any others that were already having to do it) will now have to attend the first weekend to collect a potential trophy:


Jason Bateman (supporting actor for “DTF St. Louis” and director nominee for “Black Rabbit”; EP on “DTF St. Louis”)
Charles Melton (supporting actor; EP on “Beef”)
Dakota Fanning (supporting actress for “All Her Fault”; EP on “Margo’s Got Money Troubles”)
David Harbour (supporting actor; EP on “DTF St. Louis”)
Nick Offerman (supporting actor for “Death by Lightning”; supporting actor for “Margo’s Got Money Troubles”)
Lee Sung Jin (director and writer for “Beef”; EP on “Beef”)
Steven Conrad (writing; EP on “DTF St. Louis”)
Megan Gallagher (writing; EP on “All Her Fault”)
Gabe Rotter and Daniel Pearle (writing nominees and EPs on “The Beast in Me”)



	Names like Martin Short (game show host; lead actor and EP for “Only Murders in the Building”) and Conan O’Brien (writing and variety special nominee for “The Oscars”; EP on “The Beast in Me”) were already committed for two weekends.

	From the outside, you’d assume any nominee wants to show up, celebrate and soak in the festivities. But celebrities and industry professionals lead busy lives, including many with children. For some, Labor Day weekend is the last chance for a trip or a backyard barbecue before the kids head back to school. Now it’s off the calendar. And not everyone lives in Los Angeles.
	

	It also collides with two major film festivals. Venice and Telluride will both be underway that first weekend. By the time the Emmys arrive Monday, Sept. 14, the Toronto International Film Festival will be in full swing.

	From a studio and network standpoint, that’s two straight weekends of planning, glam and everything else that goes into getting talent camera-ready for the big show.

	In recent years, the two Creative Arts nights have been edited into a tape-delayed special on FXX. Talks are ongoing to do the same with these first two nights, but nothing has been confirmed. So picture it: Harbour, a cultural fixture since “Stranger Things” and now doing career-best work in “DTF St. Louis,” finally wins his much-deserved statuette, and no one at home can watch it happen. What if “Beef” double nominee and supporting actor star Charles Melton makes history as the first Asian winner of the supporting actor (limited) field? Is that not worth seeing live?



	

									Michael Buckner
	



	“I don’t know how the Emmys fucked this up so bad,” one awards strategist tells Variety. “The move doesn’t make much sense, and all they’ve done is piss off a lot of the people and stars they are trying to court and attend their ceremony. I think this will blow up in their face.”

	Now listen, and I must admit, there are far bigger, more pressing issues going on in the world aside from Hollywood people giving up their Labor Day weekend to go to an awards ceremony.

	But we are also missing the opportunity to witness some historic moments.

	In 2023, “Beef” writer-director-producer Lee Sung Jin swept, winning for writing, directing and the limited series itself. He remains one of only a handful of Asian filmmakers to hold an Emmy for directing in any genre category, alongside Tony To, who shared the prize with seven others for the 2002 miniseries “Band of Brothers,” and Hwang Dong-hyuk, who won for the first season of drama “Squid Game” in 2022. Would this decision have gone down so easily if past winners like Steven Zaillian (“Ripley”) or Mike White (“The White Lotus”) were in the running this year?

	Lee is also up for writing in a limited series, a category only seven people have ever won more than once: Jane Anderson, Charlie Brooker, James Costigan, Andrew Davies, William Hanley, Abby Mann and Tracy Keenan Wynn. The prospect of the first nonwhite writer accepting that award off-air is deflating.
	

	The artisans already struggle to get the coverage they deserve. What many don’t realize is that the Creative Arts ceremonies have no red carpet. Nominees file in and take their seats with no chance to stop for interviews or share their excitement. And if the Academy suddenly adds a carpet to the second night, how insulting is that to everyone who could have walked it in years past? Good luck, too, getting press to surrender their Labor Day weekend to cover it. Most awards pundits are already out of town at Telluride and Venice.

	Those two Creative Arts nights are where guest actors, costume designers, composers and editors get to own the headlines for a day. Now those headlines will be swallowed by A-listers like David Harbour and Linda Cardellini, who land in the same ceremony. Think about what that does to the self-submitting actors from the medical drama “The Pitt,” Brittany Allen and Jeff Kober, who could have generated real headlines and career-making moments.

	So what’s the rationale, according to the TV Academy?

	“We’re really excited to flex more creativity into the show,” TV Academy chair Cris Abrego tells Variety. “This is not new, but the Academy’s always been committed to delivering an Emmy telecast that celebrates television, and also creating the best possible viewing experience. But it needs time, and that’s where we really want to find time to create an incredible night of entertainment that celebrates all of the Emmy winners.”

	In picking these five categories, Abrego says the goal was to spread the change across disciplines, including performers, writers and directors. “There’s no perfect solution because every category obviously represents extraordinary work, but we really wanted just to take an approach to be as balanced and as equitable as possible,” he says. “So then, what we really did was rather than concentrating on the change in one discipline or one peer group, it’s a small number of categories across the performances, writing and directing.”

	The DGA, SAG-AFTRA and the WGA issued a joint statement expressing their displeasure with the decision to move five categories off the NBC telecast: “The Emmys exist to celebrate the best of television and the artists who are central to bringing these stories to life. The decision to eliminate these categories from the primetime broadcast devalues the contributions of the talented people the Academy is meant to honor. An awards show dedicated to recognizing excellence should not reduce recognition for the artists whose work gives it meaning.”
	

	God bless Mariska Hargitay, but plenty of industry analysts scratched their heads when she was announced as host. What will the extra 20 to 30 minutes actually be used for? They say comedic bits or musical acts, but that hardly seems like justification. Are they going to put the original music and lyrics winner onstage? Will they have a live rendition of The Rembrandts singing the “Friends” theme “I’ll Be There for You?” Sounds like fun, but is it worth kicking five categories off the telecast?

	Sources say the reaction from networks and studios was blunt. Notable executives such as Netflix’s Bela Bajaria and HBO’s Casey Bloys reportedly made pointed calls to the TV Academy to voice their disdain, especially since they are most affected, having the most nominees across those categories. According to multiple sources, the TV Academy is not planning to back down — it is digging in and moving forward.

	NBC is hosting this year’s telecast, and surely, it will want to put on the best show possible, but this was (is) a lose-lose situation. Just look at others that have tried this.

	In February 2022, the Oscars pulled eight categories from the live telecast, handing them out an hour early and editing the speeches back into the show. The industry and awards enthusiasts hated it. Now all categories are handed out live in the same broadcast. The Golden Globes took heat last year for shunting a single category, original score, to a commercial break. It went to “Sinners.”

	While many like myself feel passionate about every artist getting their due, I also see the issues laid out in front of them. Casey Bloys, chairman and CEO of HBO Max told Variety on nomination morning when asked about the telecast what he thought the best option would be for Emmys moving forward: “I really don’t know. I think that obviously award shows have been trying to figure it out. I think the Grammys have done a really nice job in terms of adapting over the years, and I know everybody’s trying really hard. I don’t know the right answer.”

	The Emmys have more than 115 categories, and handing them all out in one live ceremony isn’t realistic. To be fair, the Grammys’ main telecast presents only nine out of 96 categories, recently expanded to 101, with the rest given out earlier that afternoon at the Grammy Premiere ceremony. The Emmys may look like the show is mimicking that model. But if you’re going to cut, why not streamline the whole thing?
	



	

					Stephen Colbert and Jimmy Kimmel at the 2019 Emmys.
									Getty Images
	



	Consider that writing for a variety series is the lone moved category where the nominated staffs of “Jimmy Kimmel Live,” “Last Week Tonight with John Oliver” and “The Late Show with Stephen Colbert” are in the mix. I’d wager that if Lorne Michaels’ “Saturday Night Live” were on that list, the category wouldn’t have budged.

	Then there’s the outstanding variety series race, which stays on the telecast but has become its own puzzle. Earlier this year, the Academy again merged the talk and scripted variety categories into one, a move meant to solve a long-running conundrum as the pool of eligible late-night and sketch shows keeps shrinking. But the fix created a new headache. As part of the same rule change, outstanding variety series is now an “area” award, meaning the nominees aren’t really competing against one another. Each is independently trying to get at least 90% of voters to answer “yes” to a single question: “Does this nominee merit an Emmy?” Clear that bar and the show wins, regardless of whether another one does. If no nominee hits 90%, the highest “yes” percentage takes it. There is always at least one winner, and there could be several.

	The nominees: Comedy Central’s “The Daily Show,” ABCs “Jimmy Kimmel Live,” HBO Max’s “Last Week Tonight with John Oliver,” CBS’s “The Late Show with Stephen Colbert” and NBC’s “Saturday Night Live.”

	So here’s the math the Academy may not have considered: if all five clear the bar, that’s five winners and five acceptance speeches. The 25 to 30 minutes of the telecast that producers hoped to get back are now … gone.

	If the goal is saving time, I think there are better paths: Streamline the disciplines, keeping the presented categories consistent one year, then rotate which ones move the next. The other is moving the entire genre field, and not just fragments. If most variety categories already exist in the Creative Arts, move them all. Stop splitting the race down the middle. Why is a director (drama) nominee more important than a director (reality)? Treat them as equal disciplines.

	No matter what, the Academy has a real problem to solve, but I’m not sure this was the answer.

schema.headline: The Emmys ‘F—ed This Up’: Streamers and Networks Are Outraged as Five Categories Get Cut From the Telecast

title: Adam Driver Turns Down So Many Marvel Movie Offers That It’s ‘Become a Tradition’ at the Studio to ‘Zoom With Him and Then He Passes’
description: Marvel Studios president Kevin Feige revealed on the “Happy Sad Confused” podcast that Adam Driver has repeatedly turned down offers to join the Mar
body:
Marvel Studios president Kevin Feige revealed on the “Happy Sad Confused” podcast that Adam Driver has repeatedly turned down offers to join the Marvel Cinematic Universe over the years.

	“It’s no secret we’d like to [work with Adam],” Feige replied. “But it’s a tradition to Zoom with him and then he passes. That’s a long-lived tradition. He couldn’t be more cordial. He is usually upfront about how he is feeling.”


	Driver already has one Hollywood mega-franchise under his belt thanks to his role as Kylo Ren/Ben Solo in three “Star Wars” movies, so it’s understandable why he’d be super selective when it comes to adding the MCU to his filmography. Social media is currently rife with rumors about the Oscar nominee being courted to play Magneto in Marvel’s “X-Men” reboot. Studio boss Kevin Feige said on the podcast that no “X-Men” casting choices have been set at this time. He also did not reveal which roles Driver has passed on.
	

	If Driver had his way, he would’ve continued in the “Star Wars” universe with “The Hunt for Ben Solo.” The actor notably revealed to the AP last fall that he worked with director Steven Soderbergh on developing a sequel movie centered on his fan favorite character, only for Disney executives to shoot the movie down.

	“I always was interested in doing another ‘Star Wars,’” Driver said at the time. “I had been talking about doing another one since 2021. Kathleen [Kennedy] had reached out. I always said: ‘With a great director and a great story, I’d be there in a second.’ I loved that character and loved playing him.”
	

	Driver continued, “We presented the script to Lucasfilm. They loved the idea. They totally understood our angle and why we were doing it. We took it to Bob Iger and Alan Bergman, and they said no. They didn’t see how Ben Solo was alive. And that was that.”

schema.headline: Adam Driver Turns Down So Many Marvel Movie Offers That It’s ‘Become a Tradition’ at the Studio to ‘Zoom With Him and Then He Passes’

title: In-N-Out Fight Breaks Out Between Employee & Customer, on Video!
description:
In-N-Out Brawl
                          Fists Flying Faster Than the Food!!!
body:
In-N-Out Brawl
                          Fists Flying Faster Than the Food!!!
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 20, 2026
                            5:53 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            It looks like In-N-Out added a late-night beat-down to their secret menu, as viral videos show an employee and a customer going at it.
      
    
            The burger joint brawl broke out at one of the chain's Sacramento locations early Saturday morning, after a customer was allegedly disrespectful to an employee.
      
    
        Waiting for your permission to load the Twitter Tweet.
      
    
            Peep the clip ... both people are swinging, but the employee is the clear frontrunner in this fight. Her coworkers had to pull her off the patron in order to break it up.
      

            At one point the video pans away to show a wig on the floor, as someone seems to yell "throw your wig out, bitch."
      
    
        Waiting for your permission to load the Twitter Tweet.
      
    
            An employee then tries to throw something at the customer from behind the register. It's hard to tell what exactly was thrown.
      
    
            This isn't even the first fight In-N-Out's seen recently ... another scuffle went down at an Arizona location just last week ... talk about getting your In-N-Out "Animal Style!"

title: Diddy Wants Woman Who Sued Him to Pay for 'Frivolous' Lawsuit
description:
Diddy
                          Wants Woman Who Sued Him to Foot the Bill
body:
Diddy
                          Wants Woman Who Sued Him to Foot the Bill
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 20, 2026
                            8:55 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Diddy says one of the lawsuits against him is so baseless that he's now asking a judge to make the woman who sued him ... and her lawyers ... to pay up.
      
    
            According to court docs obtained by TMZ, Diddy is asking a federal judge to sanction Grace O'Marcaigh and her attorneys, claiming they filed what he calls a frivolous amended complaint against him even after a judge ruled California courts don't have personal jurisdiction over him.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            In the filing, Diddy's lawyers argue O'Marcaigh's amended complaint simply repackaged the same jurisdictional arguments the court had already rejected instead of fixing the problems identified by the judge.
      

            They say O'Marcaigh and her lawyers ignored the court's ruling, forcing Diddy to spend more money defending what they call a meritless case. He's asking the judge to hit them with sanctions, including attorneys' fees and costs.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            O'Marcaigh's attorneys disagree. In letters attached to the filing, they argue the amended complaint added new allegations they believe tie Combs to California through the yacht charter at the center of the lawsuit. They also say the judge gave them permission to amend the complaint and insist the sanctions request is without merit.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            As TMZ previously reported, O'Marcaigh sued Diddy and his son, Christian "King" Combs, over an alleged 2022 incident aboard a yacht in the Caribbean. She claims Christian sexually assaulted her while she worked as a steward aboard the vessel and alleges Sean bears civil responsibility for what happened.
      
    
            Sean has denied the allegations.
      
      
              
            
              Related articles

title: D4vd Asks to Have Handcuffs Removed for Preliminary Hearing
body:
D4vd
                          Uncuff Me!!!
                      
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            D4vd's asking if he can ditch his handcuffs for his preliminary hearing in Celeste Rivas' murder case.
      
    
            According to NBC 4 Los Angeles, the singer filed a motion requesting his cuffs and other restraints be removed for the prelim, which is set to start tomorrow.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            D4vd's attorney, Blair Berk, said in the motion that he's never tried to escape or been disruptive, arguing that his 5 court appearances have all been uneventful.
      

            Prosecutors will be presenting evidence in court -- some of which is expecting to be pretty graphic -- as they try to convince a judge there's enough to warrant a murder trial.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            As you know, D4vd was arrested and charged with the first-degree murder back in April, 7 months after the teen's body was found dismembered in a Tesla registered to him.
      
    
            He was also charged with lewd and lascivious acts with a child under 14 ... as well as mutilating a body.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            The prosecution claims D4vd started having a sexual relationship with Celeste when she was just 13 years old.
      
    
            They say they have DNA evidence, surveillance footage, text messages, and phone records that indicate the musician is responsible for the young girl's horrific death.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            D4vd has pleaded not guilty and been in custody since he was arrested earlier this year.
      
    
            As of right now, Los Angeles Superior Court Judge Charlaine Olmedo hasn't responded to his unshackling motion.
      
      
              
            
              Related articles
